as promised, here is more on photonics 

1 with buy signals, 7 with sell signals

Sector running hot. Signals have to scream Sell due to overextended. But a 'Sell' signal doesn't always mean a crash; sometimes it just means a rest stop. It’s possible the engines are just cooling down before the next leg up. The trend isn't broken … til proven otherwise

$MRVL , $AAOI , $AEHR , $LPTH, $LWLG , $UMC , $NOK , 
$SIVEF

text:
I’ll give you two. The MORAN AI Bottleneck Index says: 👇 

The booming AI buildout has 2 large bottlenecks which lie in Photonics/Optics & Memory. 

*Roundhill Memory ETF (DRAM)*
- Holds giant memory names: 
Samsung, SK-Hynix, Micron, Sandisk. 

*Fabrinet (FN)*
- Largest Optics packaging for AI boom.

If you want the full MORAN terminal, DM me. It’s better than Bloomberg’s terminal & they don’t measure AI’s Bottlenecks like this. 

The next major bottleneck is Power (problems already emerging). We will need to take energy intensive compute to orbit to alleviate that. 

Counting on SpaceX for bringing Starship (fully reusable rocket w/ 4x more payload than Falcon 9) online with higher launch cadence. 🚀

text:
Which earnings are you looking forward to most this week?

We’re getting major updates across several key trends:
1. Photonics/Networks: $GLW, $CLS, $APH
2. Memory: $STX, $SNDK, $WDC
3. Grid/Energy: $BE, $XOM, $CVX
4. Finance: $HOOD, $SOFI
5. Magnificent 7: $META, $AMZN, $GOOGL, $MSFT, $AAPL
6. AI Software: $ZETA, $LMND

Very exciting week ahead. 

If Trump doesn’t do anything out of the box, it looks like we’re in for a really strong week for the S&P 500.

I’ll be paying especially close attention to sentiment in the statements, execution, forward guidance, and industry insights - particularly in Memory, Photonics, and Energy. 

My top focus: $SNDK, $GLW, and $BE.

Some analysts are calling for 40% revenue growth at $ZETA - really curious to see if they deliver.

On $HOOD and $SOFI, I’m not expecting extraordinary results. Robinhood in particular remains heavily tied to crypto market volatility and success which has recently been low.

As for the Mag 7, five of them report this week - crazy!

I’ll be watching cloud revenue growth closely at $AMZN, $GOOG, and $MSFT. 

For $META, I want updates on AI applications across their business segments, AI project progress, and ad spend trends. 

For $AAPL, I’m looking for order numbers on their recent product launches plus any fresh details on AI projects and integrations.

text:
Amkor $AMKR is currently playing out a similar pattern to many other photonics/optics/AI supply chain companies; the Bull Flag breakout!

After months of consolidation we’ve seen a huge breakout over the last few weeks, but where do we go from here?

$AMKR is currently up about 98% YTD and is looking stretched now on almost all timeframes.

Short term; $AMKR likely needs to consolidate for a few weeks, to cool down, allow the moving averages to catch up, for institutions to accumulate more shares and to build momentum for another move up.

Fundamentally; $AMKR is the go-to advanced packaging supplier for both $AVGO and $TSMC - if Amkor can continue scaling production, expect this to continue to feed through into revenues (and profits) and further long term growth.

If $AMKR can continue to execute, their stock will continue to climb long term.

As AI clusters scale, copper hits physical limits and the next bottleneck becomes optical infrastructure. Here are 15 names positioned for it: 1. $LITE owns the laser + optical switching side of the trade and is one of the cleanest pure plays on AI optical demand. 2. $COHR wins from lasers, modules, and networking hardware that power hyperscale AI infrastructure and cloud expansion. 3. $AAOI is one of the best ways to play AI optical transceivers with major hyperscaler demand for 800G and 1.6T connectivity. 4. $SIVE benefits from the push toward faster semiconductor-to-optical integration as AI infrastructure scales. 5. $MRVL controls a huge part of the DSP + interconnect story with optical networking chips and high-speed connectivity. 6. $AVGO sits at the center of AI networking through switching, custom silicon, and optical interconnect demand. 7. $ANET is the Ethernet backbone moving massive AI workloads across hyperscale clusters and data centers. 8. $GLW supplies the specialty glass + fiber needed for the optical transport layer behind AI infrastructure. 9. $JBL benefits from building and scaling the actual hardware behind networking systems and optical modules. 10. $AEHR wins from burn-in + testing demand as AI ASICs and high-power optical hardware move into production. 11. $POET is focused on lower-cost optical engines designed to improve efficiency inside AI data centers. 12. $LWLG is pushing next-gen polymer photonics that could make optical communication faster and more efficient. 13. $QCLS brings exposure to advanced laser systems supporting precision photonics and next-gen optical demand. 14. $LPTH provides specialty optics and photonic components tied to industrial, defense, and AI-driven systems. 15. $ALAB gives exposure to the connectivity + infrastructure side helping AI clusters scale faster. I’ll only say this once.
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang just revealed the ‘Five Layer’ AI Model.
These 5 layers include;
1. Energy - $BE $OKLO $VRT
2. Compute - $NBIS $IREN $CIFR
3. Photonics - $AAOI $LITE $SIVE
4. Memory - $MU $SNDK $STX
5. Chips - $NVDA $AMD $AVGO

All of these stocks will explode in 2026 & 2027 as AI continued to expand.
Tell me, which stock are you most bullish on?

text: Huge beat on $QCOM could be signaling the bottom of the Smartphone Market, where it makes up a third of $GFS Revenue, could explain part of the surge in Volume seen on their OBV chart, instead of just a bet on the Photonics market; $GFS OBV has once again finished above it's Dec'22 ATH level, if it continues holding above past earnings, this stock will see ATHs again soon.
embedded_text:
If $QCOM sees a bottom in smartphone, it means there is also a bottom for $SOI and $GFS's core business.

If these see the weakest part of their business rebound while the bull thesis relies on photonics, there's no limit to what I'll tell my boss in a few months. https://t.co/KGNaFypf4D

For the first time, researchers at QuiX Quantum have demonstrated below threshold error mitigation in a photonic quantum computer. They used a technique called photon distillation, where multiple imperfect photons are combined through quantum interference to produce a cleaner, more identical photon. On a 20-mode photonic processor, this method reduced photon indistinguishability errors by 2.2× and achieved a 1.2× net reduction in total system error. That matters because quantum systems usually introduce more errors than they fix. This flips that balance. It could significantly reduce the number of photon sources needed, potentially by up to 4×, making photonic quantum computers far more practical 👀 In other words, this approach improves qubit quality at the source, bringing scalable, light based quantum computing much closer to reality."}}}, "rest_id": "2049521863710405103", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1785", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2049623614115246266", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "28", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
CPO, SILICON PHOTONICS, AND OPTICAL INTERCONNECT

CPO IS MOVING FROM R&D TO EARLY PRODUCTION, BUT THE NEAR-TERM DOLLARS REMAIN SMALL RELATIVE TO THE STRATEGIC SIGNAL (READ-THROUGH 10)

Call support: Management said FormFactor is “now beginning to ramp our TRITON production test system, co-developed with Advantest and Tokyo Electron,” and that the ramp is accelerating. Management attributed the acceleration to “growing volumes of CPO chips planned for later this year” and FormFactor’s leadership in Test Insertion 1, which ensures known-good die on the photonic integrated circuit wafer. Management also cautioned that CPO remains in “the very early innings” and that other insertions are “proving to be difficult for customers to implement in production.”

Affected companies: Advantest Corporation (6857: Japan) and Tokyo Electron Ltd. (8035: Japan) are positively affected with low near-term magnitude and higher long-duration optionality. The transmission mechanism is direct: TRITON was co-developed with these partners, and early CPO production test creates future attach opportunities for ATE, probers, and co-developed test platforms.

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO: US), Marvell Technology Inc. (MRVL: US), Coherent Corp. (COHR: US), Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE: US), and Fabrinet (FN: US) are positively affected with low near-term but potentially meaningful long-duration magnitude. The mechanism is validation of silicon photonics and CPO moving toward production use cases in AI networking. If CPO becomes necessary to address bandwidth density and power constraints, optical component suppliers, silicon photonics developers, and AI networking ASIC vendors benefit from a larger production ecosystem.

Teradyne Inc. (TER: US) is mixed to modestly positive. Management emphasized an open ecosystem and specifically said FormFactor works closely with Teradyne, but the named TRITON co-development partners were Advantest and Tokyo Electron. The mechanism is that Teradyne can still participate in future CPO insertions, but the earliest named production-test system appears more closely tied to Advantest and Tokyo Electron.

Near-term trading catalyst: positive for CPO sentiment but limited in revenue magnitude. The call supports the view that CPO is no longer purely a lab topic, but it does not indicate that CPO is yet a large 2026 revenue category for the broader supply chain.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: potentially high. The critical point is that Insertion 1 is focused on validating known-good photonic integrated circuits before expensive downstream packaging steps. If this becomes a standard production insertion, optical probing and electro-optical test can become recurring infrastructure layers in AI networking manufacturing.

DOWNSTREAM HARDWARE AND OEMS

AI SUPPLY-CHAIN TIGHTNESS IS POSITIVE FOR SEMICONDUCTOR VENDORS BUT NEGATIVE FOR HARDWARE OEM MARGIN FLEXIBILITY (READ-THROUGH 11)

Call support: Management described DRAM as supply constrained, HBM demand stepping up, networking demand strong, data-center CPU probe demand improving, CPO chip volumes planned later in 2026, and probe-card capacity constraints across FormFactor and competitors. The call also noted isolated cases where customers are willing to pay expedite fees in a capacity-constrained environment.

Affected companies: Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL: US), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Co. (HPE: US), Super Micro Computer Inc. (SMCI: US), Lenovo Group Ltd. (0992: Hong Kong), and HP Inc. (HPQ: US) are negatively affected with low-to-moderate magnitude on margin and delivery-risk grounds, while remaining positively exposed to AI server demand. The transmission mechanism is input-cost and availability pressure. If DRAM, HBM, probe cards, and advanced-packaging capacity remain tight, OEMs and server integrators face higher component costs, longer lead times, and reduced ability to optimize gross margin.

Apple Inc. (AAPL: US) is modestly negatively affected through memory availability and potential LPDDR/DRAM cost pressure. The mechanism is less directly AI-server related but still relevant if DRAM wafer starts are dynamically allocated across HBM, DDR5, DDR4, and other designs to maximize supplier margin.

Near-term trading catalyst: negative for gross-margin assumptions at hardware OEMs if memory price increases and AI component shortages persist.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: mixed. AI infrastructure demand supports server revenue growth, but the profit pool may continue migrating upstream toward memory suppliers, advanced-packaging providers, foundries, and test suppliers rather than downstream assemblers and OEMs.

CONSUMER SEMICONDUCTOR RECOVERY IS NOT THE DRIVER; AI INFRASTRUCTURE IS ABSORBING THE INCREMENTAL TEST AND CAPACITY DOLLARS (READ-THROUGH 12)

Call support: Management described Q2 Foundry & Logic growth as driven primarily by data-center CPU strength, with continued networking strength and “steady demand in PC and mobile.” The word “steady” is important because it contrasts with the stronger language used for HBM, networking, GPUs, custom ASICs, and CPO.

Affected companies: Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM: US), MediaTek Inc. (2454: Taiwan), Qorvo Inc. (QRVO: US), Skyworks Solutions Inc. (SWKS: US), and other mobile-exposed semiconductor suppliers are neutral to modestly negatively affected from a relative-growth perspective. The transmission mechanism is capital and capacity allocation. The call suggests incremental advanced test and probe demand is being pulled toward AI infrastructure rather than broad PC or mobile demand acceleration.

Apple Inc. (AAPL: US), HP Inc. (HPQ: US), and Lenovo Group Ltd. (0992: Hong Kong) are neutral to modestly negatively affected as end-market read-throughs. The mechanism is that upstream test demand is not indicating a strong consumer device rebound; PC and mobile are stable contributors, not the source of upside.

Near-term trading catalyst: negative for broad consumer semiconductor beta if investors are looking for evidence of a synchronized semiconductor recovery. The FormFactor call supports a narrower AI-led cycle.

Longer-duration fundamental shift: positive for AI infrastructure concentration and negative for the relative importance of consumer electronics in leading-edge semiconductor capital allocation. The call reinforces that the incremental complexity dollars are accruing to HBM, AI networking, CPU inference, GPU, custom ASIC, CPO, and advanced packaging rather than to traditional PC/mobile cycles.

text:
#SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia

Take a moment to read this, friends — we’re going to talk about the unknown side of Sivers.

A lot of investors — and me first — only knew Sivers for its photonics business (the lasers for AI datacenters). 

It’s exciting and has massive potential. But after digging a bit deeper, we discover a real hidden gem: the Wireless division.

 It still accounts for ~70% of current revenue, delivered very strong growth (+85% on some quarters in 2025), and provides solid cash-flow stability.

 It’s the quiet but powerful pillar that makes the entire $SIVE investment thesis much less risky.

First of all, what is a beamformer? (simple explanation) : 

Imagine you want to send a super-fast internet signal (5G, future 6G, or satellite connection) to a phone, a house, or a satellite.

A regular radio signal spreads out in all directions, like a light bulb lighting up an entire room. It works, but it’s very inefficient. You waste a lot of energy, the signal weakens quickly over distance, and there are lots of interferences with other devices around.

A beamformer (literally “beam former”) is like an ultra-precise smart projector:
Instead of spreading everywhere, it concentrates all the signal power into a very narrow directional beam pointed exactly at your phone or your house.

Result? The connection is much faster, much more stable, reaches much farther… and uses way less energy.

Sivers Wireless develops exactly these mmWave beamformers (chips + antenna arrays). 
This technology is essential for high-speed 5G/6G, Fixed Wireless Access (FWA = wireless internet instead of fiber), SATCOM, and defense applications.

Major wins to date (April 2026):

• January 2025 → $5.4M contract (60 M SEK) signed with a global Tier-1 ( The crowd is murmering Nokia ? ) 

It’s for the next-generation beamforming transceivers. Even better: the collaboration is planned across multiple generations.

• New → Firm $3M purchase order with Tachyon Networks (28 GHz FWA). Production and shipments already starting in 2026 — real revenue incoming.

• 3 design wins in 5G/FWA already secured → potential of around 1 million units over the first three years of production (starting 2026).

• India → Historic partnership with Intel + WiSig Networks + office opened in Bangalore to target the massive FWA market.

• Defense → CHIPS Act funding (2nd round ongoing), contracts with Raytheon, BAE Systems, Ericsson. Daybreak™ 7-15 GHz ICs now available (March 2026) — very competitive in power and efficiency.

• SATCOM → Cloudchaser chipset + Maverick antenna arrays launched (Feb 2026) + strong commercial interest.

• 6G → Already involved in ISAC research with Nokia.

Why is this strategic for $SIVE?

Wireless brings stability and predictable revenues while the AI photonics division ramps into mass production (2026-2027). It’s the financial cushion that lets the company breathe… and it has strong potential to become a real cash cow from 2026-2028.

And even if photonics hits some delays or doesn’t deliver as expected, the Wireless division alone is strong enough to justify a major re-rating on its own.

In short: $SIVE is not just an AI laser story.

With the recent news of the Photonics spin-off to Silicon Valley, $SIVE (Sweden) is effectively becoming a pure-play leader in Wireless/SATCOM/Defense, while still holding the majority of the US AI laser powerhouse. It’s the ultimate value unlock.

It’s also a powerful play in mmWave 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense, and emerging markets — with technology that is already generating revenue.

I hope this clarified some things and/or brought new info for the Sivers community!

This is not financial advice. I’m just digging into this company and sharing what I found.

3238}, {"from_index": 3710, "richtext_types": ["Italic"], "to_index": 3800}]}, "text": "#SIVE $SIVE #5G #6G #Beamforming #FWA #SATCOM #CHIPSAct #Semiconductors #Wireless #Nokia Take a moment to read this, friends — we’re going to talk about the unknown side of Sivers. A lot of investors — and me first — only knew Sivers for its photonics business (the lasers for AI datacenters). It’s exciting and has massive potential. But after digging a bit deeper, we discover a real hidden gem: the Wireless division. It still accounts for ~70% of current revenue, delivered very strong growth (+85% on some quarters in 2025), and provides solid cash-flow stability. It’s the quiet but powerful pillar that makes the entire $SIVE investment thesis much less risky. First of all, what is a beamformer? (simple explanation) : Imagine you want to send a super-fast internet signal (5G, future 6G, or satellite connection) to a phone, a house, or a satellite. A regular radio signal spreads out in all directions, like a light bulb lighting up an entire room. It works, but it’s very inefficient. You waste a lot of energy, the signal weakens quickly over distance, and there are lots of interferences with other devices around. A beamformer (literally “beam former”) is like an ultra-precise smart projector: Instead of spreading everywhere, it concentrates all the signal power into a very narrow directional beam pointed exactly at your phone or your house. Result? The connection is much faster, much more stable, reaches much farther… and uses way less energy. Sivers Wireless develops exactly these mmWave beamformers (chips + antenna arrays). This technology is essential for high-speed 5G/6G, Fixed Wireless Access (FWA = wireless internet instead of fiber), SATCOM, and defense applications. Major wins to date (April 2026): • January 2025 → $5.4M contract (60 M SEK) signed with a global Tier-1 ( The crowd is murmering Nokia ? ) It’s for the next-generation beamforming transceivers. Even better: the collaboration is planned across multiple generations. • New → Firm $3M purchase order with Tachyon Networks (28 GHz FWA). Production and shipments already starting in 2026 — real revenue incoming. • 3 design wins in 5G/FWA already secured → potential of around 1 million units over the first three years of production (starting 2026). • India → Historic partnership with Intel + WiSig Networks + office opened in Bangalore to target the massive FWA market. • Defense → CHIPS Act funding (2nd round ongoing), contracts with Raytheon, BAE Systems, Ericsson. Daybreak™ 7-15 GHz ICs now available (March 2026) — very competitive in power and efficiency. • SATCOM → Cloudchaser chipset + Maverick antenna arrays launched (Feb 2026) + strong commercial interest. • 6G → Already involved in ISAC research with Nokia. Why is this strategic for $SIVE? Wireless brings stability and predictable revenues while the AI photonics division ramps into mass production (2026-2027). It’s the financial cushion that lets the company breathe… and it has strong potential to become a real cash cow from 2026-2028. And even if photonics hits some delays or doesn’t deliver as expected, the Wireless division alone is strong enough to justify a major re-rating on its own. In short: $SIVE is not just an AI laser story. With the recent news of the Photonics spin-off to Silicon Valley, $SIVE (Sweden) is effectively becoming a pure-play leader in Wireless/SATCOM/Defense, while still holding the majority of the US AI laser powerhouse. It’s the ultimate value unlock. It’s also a powerful play in mmWave 5G/6G, SATCOM, defense, and emerging markets — with technology that is already generating revenue. I hope this clarified some things and/or brought new info for the Sivers community! This is not financial advice. Hard ≠ unique. If hyperscalers care, they won’t single-source a tiny cap such as $SIVE

That view misses what is already happening under the surface. This is a company that has shipped tens of millions of devices and is producing at scale today. This is not a lab concept or a maybe in five years technology, it is real manufacturing with real deployment and real customers. What becomes even more interesting is where they sit in the stack. As AI infrastructure scales, the industry is running into hard physical limits, power, heat, bandwidth. One of the key architectural shifts being explored is the move toward external laser sources for optical interconnects. That is exactly where Sivers is positioned. If that approach becomes standard, they are not just another supplier, they become part of a critical bottleneck layer in next generation systems. And this is not easy technology to replicate. We are talking about sub micron manufacturing precision and extremely tight optical performance tolerances. That kind of capability is built over many years, and only a small number of companies globally can actually execute at that level. On top of that, one of the biggest risks in photonics, scaling production, has already been addressed. Through partnerships with foundries like WIN Semiconductor, there is already a path toward high volume manufacturing. That removes a major uncertainty that holds many companies in this space back. At the same time, they are quietly embedded in the global photonics ecosystem. Collaborations with research leaders like IMEC place them inside the same innovation network that feeds the broader semiconductor industry. That kind of positioning often means early access to what comes next and a role in shaping it. Put all of this together and the picture looks very different from the usual speculative small cap narrative. It starts to look more like a scaled and technically entrenched player sitting right at the intersection of where AI infrastructure is heading next."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2050272539851821157"}}, "rest_id": "2050305940516204613", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "19624", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2050316023203590144", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "456", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
The 7-Layer Photonics Stack

1.Materials & Wafers — InP, GaAs, silicon substrates. InP supply is the chokepoint.

https://t.co/X8pBSexGF9 & Epitaxy — Equipment to grow the crystal layers. MOCVD machines. Few players.

3.Lasers & Devices — VCSELs, DFB lasers, modulators. Where the photons actually get made.

4.Foundries (III-V Fabs) — The fabs that process compound semiconductors. Scarce and specialized.

5.Test & Inspection — Verify everything works at speed and scale. Lower binary risk, often overlooked.

6.Optics & Modules — Transceivers, MEMS, co-packaged optics (CPO). Where hyperscaler demand hits first.

7.Networking & Connectivity — Optical switches, DSPs, interconnect. The system layer.

text:
Ich habe die Liste geklaut, aber einfach um sicher zu gehen, dass auch in der deutschen Bubble ein paar Leute vom Thema #Photonic mitbekommen.

THE 7 LAYERS OF PHOTONICS

1. Materials & Wafers (substrate layer)
$AXTI, $IQE $WOLF, $COHR, $LWLG

2. Tools (fabrication layer)
$ASML, $AMAT, $LRCX 

3. Lasers (light generation layer)
$LITE, $COHR, $LASR, $SMTC 

4. Foundries (manufacturing layer)
$TSM, $TSEM, $GFS, $UMC, $INTC

5. Test, Inspection & Packaging (reliability layer)
$AEHR, $VIAV, $ONTO, $AMKR, $FN

6. Optics (module layer)
$AAOI, $POET, $GLW

7. Networking (connectivity layer)
$CRDO, $MRVL, $AVGO, $ANET, $CIEN

text:
c:7

$M7U (Nynomic AG) is a German micro-cap, off the beaten path, directly part of the photonics supply chain.

Here’s my simplified explanation of what this company does and why I like it:

AI datacenters need massive amounts of fiber optic connections to move data between chips at the speed of light. Those fiber optic components are manufactured inside specialized reactors made by a company called AIXTRON – which controls roughly 90% of the global market for these machines.

Nynomic owns LayTec – a company that makes the sensors that sit inside every single one of those AIXA reactors and measure what’s happening in real time during production. Without them, manufacturing yields drop dramatically. Near 1:1 attach rate on every reactor AIXA ships.

AIXA’s optics segment is their primary growth engine for 2026, guided at €520M revenue. LayTec rides that wave on every single order.

Now the valuation gap compared to its nearest competitors. $ONTO trades at ~12.7x sales. $CAMT trades at ~15x sales. Nynomic trades at ~1.5x sales. 

The market is currently treating it like a struggling industrial company because of the 2025 restructuring noise – but management guides for significant recovery in 2026 with disproportionately large profit expansion. Medium-term target is €200M revenue at 16-19% margins.

Given the current market cap, hitting those targets would likely force a massive re-rating toward the 3x–5x sales range typical for European specialized semi-cap players.

You’re essentially buying a hidden monopoly inside the hottest semiconductor supply chain in the world at a steep discount – before it has had a chance to run.

This is a position I opened on $IBKR last week. Not available on $HOOD. Looking to add more this week if the price dips or stays flat a little longer.

text:
Daily Recap: The historic run continues
- $QQQ 21.6% in 23 sessions followed by the $SPY 15.2% and the $IWM at 17%
- $XLK continues to be the market dominant group. $AAPL very near an all time high with the obvious horses being $INTC $AMD $MU. 
- Photonics work up once again after being quiet for a few weeks. $AAOI $CIEN $LITE $VIAV $LWLG $AXTI. The group is due up next for earnings this upcoming week. 
- Software $IGV is also giving the impression that we may have bottomed. Some names are already leading that group and on Thursday we had a great earnings reaction and follow through from $TWLO. $DOCN $FSLY $RNG $FIVN $BAND $NBIS ones to watch. 
- $XLE was still the leading group for the week and it's hard to write those names off when you look at the structure on many of those stocks. $CRAK $BOAT $OIH a few places to watch over the upcoming weeks. 
- $BTC $ETH stage 4B setups unfolding with $CRCL being the only real place of interest for me for now. 
- Memory names are being lead by 4 incredibly powerful trend that show no real signs of slowing. $SNDK $STX $MU $WDC. 
- We got through major earning reports last week and this week is also an important one. Focus on names gapping up on large volume and watch the for continuation setups. $MXL is a great example. 
- Keep universe lists updated and I will see you all on Monday!

text:
Memory and photonics have already run hard, but Layer 5 (Capacity Operators) has the biggest catch-up left. 🤖

$DGXX, sitting at only $240M micro-cap, looks like one of the cleanest pure-play #GPUaaS rare setups in the entire AI infrastructure stack.

Most peers are still years behind - stuck in permitting, transformer shortages, and grid upgrades.

DGXX has already completed the hard pivot: owns 70 MW approved power + commissioned ARMS 200 modular Blackwell pods at Alabama. This is literally the fastest way to turn power into revenue with scarce live Blackwell GPUaaS capacity.

Balance sheet is rock-solid: zero debt, ~$93M strong liquidity with no forced dilution. 

The stock has gone through classic micro-cap volatility. Now it sits at a clear inflection point.

First anchor GPUaaS deal signed - $19.6M SubQ AI revenue goes live May 15. This proves the model works and derisks everything. Advancing hyperscaler discussions with customers desperate for immediate capacity.

Superstar team with hyperscaler DNA:  Venkat Rangasamy (ex-Oracle AI Factories) as VP AI Infrastructure, @hansvestberg (ex-Verizon CEO) co-founding USDC, @GerardRotonda (ex-CFO Deutsche Bank) on the board. Talent this caliber only joins a microcap when they see something truly special.

The recent #USDC spinoff is brilliant and shareholder-friendly: with subsidiary entity raises dedicated capital for pods & GPUs separately - no dilution or debt for DGXX parent entity. DGXX keeps 55% ownership and the majority upside.

Roadmap with high visibility: 10 MW by Q3 2026 → ~$137–152M ARR + colocation → $11–$30/share (4-9x from here). Full 70 MW Alabama scaling → $7B–$12B+ market cap upside.

It’s not if but how fast - hyperscalers are hungry for immediately available power and capacity, and DGXX already has it. And, this is just the beginning of a multi-year AI capacity supercycle - demand for live power and GPUs will only keep growing.

$DGXX reminds me of the early-stage #IREN/#CIFR, both rerated 5-10x+ from pre-contract lows - the risk/reward looks exceptional from here, and it is still completely under the radar.

This could be us retail’s chance to get ahead before market finally connects the dots 🔥 (institutions flood in and analysts raise guidance.) 

Disclaimer: not financial advice. Always DYOR.

text:
The tools split because the physics spans 4-5 orders of magnitude in wavelength. An RF antenna is centimeters. A photonic waveguide is nanometers. 

Tackling both in one numerical simulation would be computationally nuts, so we got HFSS for RF and Lumerical for photonics.

Deep learning models can learn an embedding over EM fields that isn’t frequency-constrained.  We can tackle RF and photonics with the same model.

That’s actually what we’re building at @arenaphysica

text:
How is $KOPN weaved into all of this photonics, AI, datacenters talk?!

Kopin is a direct play on AI data center photonics / optical interconnects: 

Neural I/o repurposes Kopin’s proprietary MicroLED pixels (via their patented bi-directional NeuralDisplay architecture) as ultra-high-speed optical transceivers. It moves data using photons instead of electrons, replacing dense copper wiring and traditional laser-based systems between GPUs, processors, boards, and racks in AI/smart data centers.

This addresses the exact pain points (power, bandwidth, heat, scalability) that drive CPO demand. It promises the same (or better) performance with a fraction of the power per bit and eliminates expensive laser modules—positioning it as a disruptive, lower-power alternative/complement to the laser-heavy CPO stack ($SIVE’s InP DFB laser arrays, $POET’s interposers, etc.).

https://t.co/jZIG8z9CBJ has placed a $15M initial purchase order to fund the demonstration chipset. Kopin owns 19.9% of https://t.co/jZIG8z9CBJ and is the exclusive manufacturer of the Neural I/o chipsets. Kopin emphasizes its U.S.-based MicroLED manufacturing for secure supply chains (a big plus in AI/defense).

Stifel recently reinstated coverage with a Buy and $5.50 target; the stock has shown momentum on the news.

Kopin is entering the same optical interconnect layer for AI infrastructure with a novel MicroLED approach that sidesteps some of the traditional laser challenges. This reinforces another high-upside, U.S.-centric player in the photonics boom for AI data centers + defense. LONG $KOPN $SIVE

text:
Photonics, Optics & Memory stocks saw another massive breakout to new all time highs last week. 

Many of these stocks are up well over 100%+ year to date.

After the recent mag7 earnings reports; I think data centers/compute providers will be the next sector to run big.

$NBIS - $CRWV - $CIFR And $IREN

On the various Mag7 earnings calls, they noted their current bottleneck wasn’t the availability of chips, but the availability of compute & power/energy. 

There are smaller data center co’s like $SNLH $BITF $APLD - but the 4 I mentioned earlier are my favourites. 

Trade idea;
Wait for a breakout above previous highs for an entry, or a re-test of this descending triangle breakout.

Nobody listened to me about;

$AXTI at $26 before it flew 175% 
$AAOI at $80 and it ran 90% (so far)
$MU at $156 and it launched 200%
$SNDK at $250

Title: Utility Truck with Roof-Mounted Light Array — Photonics / LiDAR Plausibility Assessment
Timestamp: March 23, 2021 | Santa Barbara, CA (702)
Tags: #Photonics #LiDAR #UtilityVehicles #Optics #PerceptionVsReality #ForensicAnalysis
Forensic Category: Visual Assessment / Technology Plausibility

⸻

⚖️ Core Proposition

Not all high-intensity light arrays or mounted equipment indicate advanced sensing systems. However, certain configurations can overlap with technologies used in mapping, scanning, and illumination.

The task is to separate:
👉 What is commonly deployed
from
👉 What is theoretically adaptable

⸻

🔍 Observed in Image

Pickup truck with roof-mounted light bar + auxiliary lighting cluster
Trailer-mounted equipment consistent with a wood chipper / landscaping machinery
Residential driveway setting
No visibly distinct sensor housings (e.g., spinning LiDAR dome, multi-lens rig, stabilized gimbal)

⸻

🔁 Effect → Cause Analysis

Observed Effect:
Large, multi-module light array mounted above cab

Most Likely Cause (Common Use):

High-lumen LED light bars used for:
Night work visibility
Landscaping / construction operations
Off-road or utility illumination

👉 These systems can appear intense on camera due to:

Lens compression
Reflective surfaces
Sensor bloom

⸻

Secondary Consideration (Technically Plausible, but Not Evident Here):

LiDAR / photonic systems typically require:

Dedicated sensor units (often cylindrical or box modules)
Clear line-of-sight mounting
Stabilization or calibration hardware
Power + data integration (visible cabling or mounts)

👉 None of those are clearly visible in this image.

⸻

⚠️ Important Distinction

A light bar ≠ LiDAR system

However:

Some industrial vehicles can carry both lighting + sensing equipment
Advanced mapping rigs (surveying, autonomous testing) use:
LiDAR
RGB cameras
IR sensors
        —but these are visually distinct when present

⸻

🧠 Conclusion

👉 Based on visible evidence, this appears to be a standard utility/work truck with high-output lighting, not a dedicated photonic or LiDAR deployment system.

👉 While dual-use adaptation is theoretically possible in broader contexts, there is no clear visual confirmation of LiDAR or advanced sensing hardware in this instance.

⸻

📌 Provenance Note

Visual interpretation grounded in:

Known vehicle equipment configurations
Optical behavior of high-intensity LEDs
Standard LiDAR hardware characteristics

text:
$SIVE 
InP100 is a standardized platform of indium phosphide (InP) lasers and amplifier chips designed for direct integration with silicon photonics.

On‑chip light source: Enables placing the laser directly on the photonic die instead of in a separate package, reducing size, cost, and optical loss.

High single‑mode power: Delivers meaningful continuous‑wave output with narrow linewidth for better reach and signal quality.

Wafer‑scale flip‑chip readiness: Engineered for reproducible, high‑precision flip‑chip bonding to silicon or SiN waveguides.

Manufacturing friendly: Standardized epi processes and on‑wafer coatings support high yield and fast scale‑up to volume production.

Flexible form factors: Available as single emitters or compact arrays to suit different channel and power needs.

Core benefit: compact, lower‑cost, and scalable photonic systems with true on‑chip light sources, provided thermal and optical interfaces are co‑designed during integration.

text:
A lot of people still think $SIVE is just another early stage photonics story.

That view misses what is already happening under the surface. This is a company that has shipped tens of millions of devices and is producing at scale today. 

This is not a lab concept or a maybe in five years technology, it is real manufacturing with real deployment and real customers. 

What becomes even more interesting is where they sit in the stack. As AI infrastructure scales, the industry is running into hard physical limits, power, heat, bandwidth. One of the key architectural shifts being explored is the move toward external laser sources for optical interconnects. That is exactly where Sivers is positioned. If that approach becomes standard, they are not just another supplier, they become part of a critical bottleneck layer in next generation systems. 

And this is not easy technology to replicate. We are talking about sub micron manufacturing precision and extremely tight optical performance tolerances. That kind of capability is built over many years, and only a small number of companies globally can actually execute at that level. On top of that, one of the biggest risks in photonics, scaling production, has already been addressed. 

Through partnerships with foundries like WIN Semiconductor, there is already a path toward high volume manufacturing. That removes a major uncertainty that holds many companies in this space back. At the same time, they are quietly embedded in the global photonics ecosystem. Collaborations with research leaders like IMEC place them inside the same innovation network that feeds the broader semiconductor industry. 

That kind of positioning often means early access to what comes next and a role in shaping it. 

Put all of this together and the picture looks very different from the usual speculative small cap narrative. 

It starts to look more like a scaled and technically entrenched player sitting right at the intersection of where AI infrastructure is heading next.

text:
$SIVE 
The market underestimates when deep tech IP actually starts to monetize. That’s exactly what we’re seeing with Sivers Semiconductors: years of investment in laser and photonics R&D that are only now reaching a scaling phase with CPO (co-packaged optics) and AI data center demand. Retail tends to give up during the long wait, while institutions step in right when visibility improves.

The “playbook” you’re describing isn’t imaginary. With Soitec and IQE, we saw similar setups: weak sentiment, skeptical analyst coverage, followed by accumulation from large players like Morgan Stanley or Point72 before a major rerating.

text:
$COHR is stepping into a new era while the market sleeps on its potential…

You might have missed it, but Coherent recently demonstrated groundbreaking silicon photonics tech with Tower Semiconductor — a 400 Gbps modulator aimed at powering next-gen AI data centers. Plus, their strategic tie with NVIDIA is set to scale optics for advanced architectures. This isn’t just another upgrade; it’s changing how data flows.

The demand is legit. After that breakthrough in silicon carbide epitaxy capabilities, which can handle power devices up to 10 kilovolts, the stock surged by 9%. Analysts are starting to notice, and the incremental $20 billion addressable market signals that Coherent isn’t just in optical communications anymore; they’re expanding quickly into high-margin sectors.

Look, Coherent isn’t just competing in the optics space; they sit at a critical bottleneck. There are only a handful of players with real expertise in silicon photonics and high-voltage power solutions — names like Lumentum and Applied Optoelectronics can’t touch this level. Their work underpins everything the AI ecosystem needs to run efficiently, making them vital as orders pile up.

If they keep landing these partnerships, the stock won't just stabilize; it’ll be poised for a repricing as more industry shifts gear. Not many are ready for this narrative shift — people are still focused on the surface layer.

Called this before the move. If you’ve been following closely, this one should already be on your radar.

https://t.co/oVlp1VlHqQ has the rest of the watchlist. Follow + bookmark.

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "243357", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2050295351144333712", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "312", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$QCOM is asymmetrical because it's being priced to fail.

The demand for CPUs is exploding and $ARM $INTC $AMD are the biggest winners.

But remember in February comparing $AAOI --> $LITE $COHR?  It was an underdog in photonics and then the market acknowledged it had the assets to be a credible 3rd place and deserved a higher MC.

$QCOM could be similar in that they have a credible set of assets to getting in on a different supercycle: CPUs.

This is intriguing because it trades at 12x forward PE. $ARM is 110x. $INTC is 125x. $AMD is 40x.

In 2021 Qualcomm bought Nuvia for $1.4B, the ex-Apple silicon team that designed the M-series cores which are the gold standard. At Computex 2025 they announced NVLink Fusion with Nvidia which means Qualcomm CPUs could slot into AI factories the same way Xeon does today.

On the Q3 FY25 call, Amon disclosed "advanced discussions with a leading hyperscaler" and guided data center revenue starting fiscal 2028. 

Three products are coming: a CPU, accelerators, and a full rack. The $2.4B Alphawave acquisition provides the high-speed connectivity IP to ship it.

Also in January 2025 they hired Sailesh Kottapalli, former chief architect of Intel Xeon.

Wednesday's earnings is the swing factor. Name the hyperscaler or guide data center revenue separately and there's some juice. 

At 12x forward, zero data center upside is priced in so there is clear potential for multiple expansion.

text:
NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang talked with Dwarkesh Patel that he believes that the way to grow AI is to continue to support the AI ecosystem across all facets.

"The investments that we've done with Lumentum and Coherent and all of the silicon photonics ecosystem, the last several years, we really reshaped the ecosystem and the supply chain of silicon photonics...

We're preparing the supply chain through invention of new technologies, new workflows, new testing equipment, double-sided probing, investing in companies, helping them scale up their capacity."

- CEO of @nvidia Jensen Huang on the @Dwarkesh Podcast

What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once.

That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me.

Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another https://t.co/JW7LUf4P2u

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2439}, {"from_index": 2465, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2499}]}, "text": "What makes a company truly compelling is not just what it is today, but how many powerful trends it could end up riding at once. That is a big part of why $SIVE / $SIVEF stands out to me. Most investors build exposure one theme at a time. They buy one company for AI, another for defense, another for satcom, another for photonics, and maybe yet another for next-generation telecom. It is a bit like assembling the future one puzzle piece at a time, hoping the picture eventually comes together. Sivers feels different. It feels more like finding one piece that already connects several of the most important parts of that picture. At first glance, it is easy to label it as just another semiconductor company. But the more I look at it, the more that feels like an oversimplification. Sivers sits in the overlap between mmWave, photonics, datacenter connectivity, satcom and defense. Those are not niche or isolated markets. They are major long-term themes, and the company has exposure to several of them at once. That is what makes the setup so compelling. Sivers does not need every single door to open at the same time for the story to work. It may be enough for one or two of these markets to accelerate, one or two partnerships to deepen, or one part of the business to gain meaningful commercial traction before investors begin to see the company in a very different light. That is why I think the “one stock, several themes” angle matters here. For a new investor, Sivers may be easier to understand if you stop thinking about it as a single narrow bet and start thinking about it as a small platform with multiple shots on goal. Not one straight road, but a crossroads. Not one engine, but several potential drivers of value that could begin contributing at different times. Of course, that does not remove the risk. Stories with several moving parts are never simple. But they can also create exactly the kind of asymmetry that makes small-cap investing so interesting, especially when the market has not fully decided how to categorize the company yet. And that is often where the upside starts to emerge. So the appeal of Sivers, to me, is not just that it has exposure to attractive end markets. It is that you do not need to buy five different companies to get that exposure. You are looking at one company that could potentially benefit from several major technology shifts at once. That kind of setup is rare. And that is why I think Sivers deserves a much closer look."}}}, "rest_id": "2045914937076183188", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "243357", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2048514663634108784", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "211", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Bottlenecks are the theme of 2026.

We’ve already had:

→ memory bottlenecks
→ photonics bottlenecks
→ CPU bottlenecks
→ GPU bottlenecks

But the next question is bigger:
What powers it all?

AI data centres don’t just need chips.
They need electricity at insane scale.

My power watchlist:

→ $CEG for nuclear
→ $VST for power generation
→ $TLN for nuclear/data centre deals
→ $GEV for turbines + grid equipment
→ $ETN for electrical infrastructure
→ $PWR for grid buildout
→ $OKLO / $SMR for SMRs

The next AI bottleneck will not be inside the data centre.
It will be the power needed to run it.

~$69M current assets

~8450 PS ratio 

(reflects extremely early revenue base, not mature scaling)

Still in commercialization / validation phase for EO polymer photonics

This is like buying $COHR at 15$ in 2014

All at a 2B market cap. 

We saw it with $LITE and $COHR — optical infrastructure compounds hard once AI data center demand shifts from narrative to structural bottleneck.

That’s the same structural lane where photonics becomes the invisible layer of compute scaling.

Not saying $LWLG becomes $COHR.

This is where those asymmetries live.

text:
First time I see someone on my feed recommending $AIP. It's nice to see different stocks than the traditional photonics / 800G plays that everybody hypes every day.

I'm also quite bullish on $AIP, although I sold my shares for a quick profit. What I really like is that they are actively decelerating their China business. It accounted for 25-30% before, which was a massive geopolitical headwind.

The downside for me is the royalty timeline. Those revenues only materialize a couple of years after the licensing deal is signed. So while I really like the stock, I'm not sure if it is the best timing to hold right now, but we'll see. The market reflects future potential, not current value so very possible it could go higher in the near term propulsed by INTC and AMD

text: I’ve had another Substac article on a photonics-related company ready for weeks, but I wanted to wait until the price was right. However, I’m starting to worry that attention for this company could increase soon and we’ve seen often enough how quickly that can gain momentum. So I’m going to publish the article even if the price isn’t right at the moment, at least not in the mid term. It would be a shame if it just ended up in the trash otherwise. And no, it’s definitely not Mycronic. This will probably be my last photonics-related Write-up for the time being.

text:
The most interesting pattern in AI photonics this year is not a fundamental story. It's a flow story. And it's already played out twice in the last six weeks. Something that popped up when I was doing research on $SIVE for an article that drops tomorrow.

$SOI Soitec. March 26: Morgan Stanley upgrades with photonics SOI as the bull case. April 22: they cross the 5% threshold and now hold 6.50% of the company, acquired through an off-market transaction. The same window saw multiple disclosed shorts from Two Sigma, Numeric, AHL Partners, and JPMorgan Asset Management.

$IQE. Up 323% in twelve months. Stockopedia tags it a Momentum Trap, trading 283% above its 200-day moving average. April 27: $MTSI MACOM leads an 81 million GBP strategic investment that ends IQE's strategic review.

The setup is the same in both names.

European compound semiconductor companies trade cheap on their home exchanges because local investors do not price the AI photonics optionality. US institutional desks notice. Analyst reports flag overvaluation. Retail and local holders sell into the perceived weakness. US institutions accumulate the float at the bottom of the shake-out. The fundamentals are not the trigger. The flow is.

Now look at $SIVE Sivers Semiconductors. Up over 900% in twelve months. Just announced a 1.6T transceiver collaboration with Jabil. Evaluating a Nasdaq New York dual listing. Stockopedia tags it a Momentum Trap. Analyst consensus 79% below market price.

Identical setup to where Soitec was in early March.

I want to be careful here. This is a hypothesis based on three data points. It tells you what could happen if the playbook repeats. It does not tell you when. It does not tell you whether the stock takes a 50% drawdown first. The companies are in different stages of maturity. Soitec has 890M EUR in revenue. Sivers has 30M USD, 72% of it NRE.

But the playbook is real. The receipts are public. It explains why analyst targets 80% below market, Momentum Trap classifications, and off-market transactions keep clustering in the same sub-sector at the same time.

I do not own any of these names. My framework is fundamentals, and I do not have edge in predicting where Morgan Stanley parks a 6.5% position three months from now. But the flow case is the most interesting non-fundamental thesis I have seen in AI infrastructure picks-and-shovels this year.

Worth watching closely.

It's a flow story. And it's already played out twice in the last six weeks. Something that popped up when I was doing research on $SIVE for an article that drops tomorrow. $SOI Soitec. March 26: Morgan Stanley upgrades with photonics SOI as the bull case. April 22: they cross the 5% threshold and now hold 6.50% of the company, acquired through an off-market transaction. The same window saw multiple disclosed shorts from Two Sigma, Numeric, AHL Partners, and JPMorgan Asset Management. $IQE. Up 323% in twelve months. Stockopedia tags it a Momentum Trap, trading 283% above its 200-day moving average. April 27: $MTSI MACOM leads an 81 million GBP strategic investment that ends IQE's strategic review. The setup is the same in both names. European compound semiconductor companies trade cheap on their home exchanges because local investors do not price the AI photonics optionality. US institutional desks notice. Analyst reports flag overvaluation. Retail and local holders sell into the perceived weakness. US institutions accumulate the float at the bottom of the shake-out. The fundamentals are not the trigger. The flow is. Now look at $SIVE Sivers Semiconductors. Up over 900% in twelve months. Just announced a 1.6T transceiver collaboration with Jabil. Evaluating a Nasdaq New York dual listing. Stockopedia tags it a Momentum Trap. Analyst consensus 79% below market price. Identical setup to where Soitec was in early March. I want to be careful here. This is a hypothesis based on three data points. It tells you what could happen if the playbook repeats. It does not tell you when. It does not tell you whether the stock takes a 50% drawdown first. The companies are in different stages of maturity. Soitec has 890M EUR in revenue. Sivers has 30M USD, 72% of it NRE. But the playbook is real. The receipts are public. It explains why analyst targets 80% below market, Momentum Trap classifications, and off-market transactions keep clustering in the same sub-sector at the same time. I do not own any of these names. My framework is fundamentals, and I do not have edge in predicting where Morgan Stanley parks a 6.5% position three months from now. But the flow case is the most interesting non-fundamental thesis I have seen in AI infrastructure picks-and-shovels this year. The AI Super Cycle rotates in phases

Semis like $ARM $AMD $INTC were Phase 1.

Money is already rotating:

Memory → $MU
Photonics / Optical → $NOK
Networking → $ANET
Compute → $IREN
Power → $VRT
Materials → $MP
Space → $ASTS
Defense / Drones → $KTOS $ONDS
Robotics → $TSLA

All you need to do is catch one part of one rotation of the cycle.

text:
No, Infleqtion and PsiQuantum are separate companies with different quantum tech approaches (neutral atoms vs. photonics).

Both are tenants at Chicago's Illinois Quantum & Microelectronics Park (IQMP). PsiQuantum is the anchor building a large photonic utility-scale system there. Infleqtion is developing its own neutral-atom quantum computer on the same campus as part of a $50M+ Illinois initiative. No direct affiliation between them.
@AIInvestorHQ Semiconductors and photonics are the future and there is nothing we can do about it

text:
Jensen's 5-Layer AI framework is the cleanest investment thesis you'll see in 2026. Energy. Compute. Photonics. Memory. Chips. Every layer is in shortage. Every layer is repricing higher.

Layer 1: Energy. AI training campuses pulling 1+ gigawatts each. Data center power demand growing 40 percent annually. Bloom Energy, Oklo, Vertiv. Bull thesis is small modular reactors and fuel cells get the AI buildout contracts. Bear thesis is grid backlogs delay the buildout 3 years.

Layer 2: Compute. Hyperscaler GPU clusters can't keep up. CoreWeave, Nebius, IREN, Cipher. The neoclouds capture overflow demand at premium pricing. Bull case is 5x revenue. Bear case is hyperscalers self-supply and squeeze them out.

Layer 3: Photonics. Networking inside data centers is the new bottleneck. Applied Optoelectronics, Lumentum, Coherent, Astera Labs. 800G and 1.6T optical transceivers sold out through 2027. Bull case is networking captures real margin. Bear case is hyperscalers vertically integrate optics.

Layer 4: Memory. The bottleneck inside the bottleneck. Micron, SanDisk, Seagate, plus SK Hynix and Samsung internationally. DA Davidson just put a $1,000 PT on Micron. Bull case is structurally extended cycle. Bear case is China NAND oversupply.

Layer 5: Chips. NVDA, AMD, Broadcom, plus custom silicon at Google, Amazon, Meta. Bull case is NVDA's CUDA moat holds 5 more years. Bear case is custom silicon takes 30 percent share by 2028 and the stack price-compresses.

What nobody's saying: this isn't a "buy everything AI" framework.

It's a structural map of where shortages compound. Every layer has supply tight through 2027. Pick the layer with the deepest moat and the cheapest valuation.

My answer: memory at Layer 4. Energy at Layer 1.

The map is real.

Pick your shovels.

text:
$AAOI took a small position. love the 20ma bounce

today: 
"wins a $21M Texas grant to expand semiconductor and photonics manufacturing in Sugar Land.  The project adds a 210K sq. ft. facility for AI data center transceiver production and is expected to create 500+ jobs." https://t.co/wKWAy76O36

Foxconn Is Ready to Embrace CPO, as Silicon Photonic Products Manage to Reach 3.2T Transmission Capacity 

On the 29th, Guo Haozhong, director of the Foxconn Semiconductor Research Institute, delivered a speech on silicon photonics trends at the 2026 MIC FORUM Spring "Intelligent New Order" seminar. He said that Foxconn's (2317) silicon photonics products can already achieve a transmission capacity of 3.2T, but with the continued shortage of InP capacity, silicon photonics will still face 10 challenges to overcome in the future.

This year, the slogan for AI servers has changed from "fiber to copper" to "fiber and copper in parallel". This is mainly because Broadcom chips have optimized the 200G transmission speed. However, copper wires will become increasingly difficult to use, especially high-speed copper wires which are heavy and require external sheathing. The advantage of optical communication is that a single wire can carry multiple wavelengths simultaneously without interfering with each other, up to a maximum of eight wavelengths.

Guo Haozhong stated that Foxconn's modulator can already achieve a single-channel transmission speed of 200G, using 2 or 4 different wavelength lasers and employing WDM (wavelength division multiplexing) technology to integrate on a PIC (photonic integrated circuit) chip; for a single-channel 200G, if two groups of four wavelengths are used, a transmission capacity of 3.2T can be achieved, and it even has the ability to upgrade the technology to 6.4T in the future.
Since future data center transmission speeds will reach over 400G, he stated that the current single-channel transmission rate of silicon photonics is limited to nearly 200G and has high power requirements. InP has advantages in high-speed transmission, not only integrating lasers but also helping to support transmission of systems above 400G. However, InP material is not as stable as silicon, resulting in low yield and high cost. Currently, due to insufficient production capacity, many companies are also investing in the research and development of other materials, such as TFLN or SOH, which have become materials of interest.

{"from_index": 2228, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2257}, {"from_index": 2423, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2467}]}, "text": "I think one of the most underestimated parts of the Sivers / $SIVE story is the SATCOM opportunity with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp. This is not just another customer relationship. Sivers has been working with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp for years through several development programs worth many millions of dollars, and the relationship appears to go much deeper than a normal supplier agreement. Sivers is part of the technology roadmap for advanced satellite terminals that are built for a world where connectivity will move across different orbits, networks and operators. That is where the potential becomes interesting. According to $SIVE own material, a SATCOM terminal can contain up to around 1,800 Sivers chips, with potential Sivers value of up to around $10,000 per terminal. That changes the way you should think about scale. This is not a consumer hardware story where millions of units are needed before revenue matters. In this case, even relatively small deployment numbers can become meaningful very quickly. If https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp sells 1,000 terminals, that is up to around $10 million for Sivers. At 5,000 terminals, up to around $50 million. At 10,000 terminals, up to around $100 million. And that is from one customer platform. This is why I think the market may be underestimating SATCOM. The focus is often on when Wireless can become meaningful again, but the answer may not only come from huge 5G volumes. It could also come from a smaller number of high-value satellite terminals where Sivers has very high value per terminal. The timing also matters. $SIVE has indicated that volume ramp is expected in H2 2026. If that happens, SATCOM moves from development revenue into something much more important: recurring product revenue tied to actual terminal deployments. And the backdrop is strong. https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp is already working with demanding customers and partners such as the US Army, NATO-related defence programs, Viasat, SES and Intelsat, while defence demand for resilient satellite communication is increasing and commercial networks are moving toward more advanced multi-orbit connectivity. Terminals need to become smarter, more capable and more complex. That is exactly where Sivers beamforming technology becomes valuable. For me, this is one of the most interesting parts of the $SIVE story into 2026. Not because it requires massive volumes. But because in SATCOM, small volumes can already become big numbers. #SATCOM #Beamforming #SatelliteCommunications #SpaceTech #DefenceTech #MultiOrbit #SIVEF"}}}, "rest_id": "2049351404595163589", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "17964", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2049540957603831884", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "629", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Laser Photonics $LASE, yaklaşık 5.7 milyon adet varantın $0.70 fiyatla kullanılmasıyla ~4 milyon dolar nakit elde etti. 

Bunun karşılığında yatırımcılara, $0.975 kullanım fiyatlı yeni varantlar (Series A-5 ve A-6) verildi.

 Bu yeni varantlar hissedar onayı sonrası devreye girecek ve 2–5 yıl vadeli olacak. 

İşlem kısa vadede nakit sağlarken, ileride hisse sulandırma riski yaratıyor.

text:
Arrogance is costly.

Some of the highly regarded Korean photonics crew are about to get punished… because they’re still looking at charts on a 15-minute / yearly basis.

https://t.co/IIoZNOe7gU

“Price is king” is a lesson they still need to learn.
Warned this guy earlier (tweet below).
But we all learn at our own pace.

The real lesson: solid analyst teams and influencers with great picks, but limited experience pumping overbought stocks to their followers right before a 40–50% correction.

$IQE is headed to the 23–28 range.
We’ll be buyers then.

text:
We will be taking a short position in Semis here.
Short trade expected to last a couple of weeks. After that, we’ll look to pick up bottoms in select photonics names following a solid correction/profit-taking phase.

Trade setup:
- Using SMH, SPXL, and SOXS
- Earnings pop into mega tech earnings today
- Ideal entry on SOXS in the $13.60–$14.25 range
- Targeting 25–30% return over the next 2 weeks

Some photonic stocks will likely sell off hard (some already started) - which should create a good buying opportunity for those who missed the big rally.

Stay tuned.

text:
The photonics bottleneck had SO many layers.

That's what it made it so damn fun.

But I think it's messed with our heads a little on FinX.

We got a bit addicted to hunting niche small cap upstream plays because there were many.

But when it comes to insane implications of the CPU bottleneck, feels like it's more about just owning the leaders.

Tell me why $INTC $ARM $AMD can't be $1T companies when GPU:CPU ratio shifts from 8:1 to 1:1. 

Literally an 8x of the TAM on what was already a $5T buildout?

text:
There is a clear relationship between photonics and SMR development. 

A major reason we began researching the photonics space on companies like $LITE $MRVL $SIVE etc. 

Laser enrichment is fundamentally a photonics system wrapped around isotope chemistry.

For SMR and HALEU supply, laser‑based schemes ( $SILXY/ $GLE, LIS‑style concepts) offer modular, potentially higher‑assay‑flexible enrichment that can be sited at smaller scale than centrifuge cascades, which fits the distributed SMR deployment thesis.

$ASPI $NNE and $LIST fit this mix as well. 

Our full report on this relationship will be sent later this week. 

Make sure you're here to receive ALL research: https://t.co/lUESQXjyp9

text:
Photonics memestocks are the meta now but feels like the big gains are behind us. Some of the popular tickers floating around are being pushed at unrealistic forward estimates imo.

I had some exposure with $GFS but been trimming after a huge run. Still plenty of juice left in broad semis, but I'm not sure they outperform indices. 

More interested in the infra layer for agents right now. That means hyperscalers and data $ORCL, platforms $COIN $NET, and hosting and control planes @AionApp.

$CRDO

Absolutely love this weekly close 

We’ve been watching this since it broke out…

Plenty of room back to ATHs & beyond at $210+

Why Silicon Photonics is the Future

Electrons = heat + power loss + speed limits.

Photons (light) = ultra-fast, low-power data movement.

Critical for:
• Exploding AI data centers
• 5G/6G & quantum communication
• Defence & high-performance computing

Intel, TSMC & Cisco are racing here. 

India just entered with sovereign capability.

Developed at MeitY-backed CoE-CPPICS, IIT Madras.

text:
He's saying the "next layer" is the deeper supply chain for plasmonics (MRVL's big bet via Polariton, not just silicon photonics).

Beyond the top names like AAOI/LITE/COHR, institutions are quietly rotating into:

- $LWLG: the electro-optic material inside the stack 
- $TSEM: the foundry where it's manufactured 
- $AEHR: the test/qualification systems 

It's early-stage, less crowded, and mirrors the first photonics wave—just ahead of the curve. The charts he posted show recent moves in TSEM/AEHR.

text:
Photonics 101 - why this sector is the next leg of AI:

Every data center being built right now has a bottleneck. It's not compute. It's the cables & connections moving data BETWEEN the chips.

Copper can't keep up. Light can.

That's photonics. Moving data through fiber & optical components instead of copper wire.

The ecosystem:

→ Lasers & light generation: $LITE $COHR $LASR
→ Optical transceivers: $AAOI $POET
→ Connectivity & DSPs: $CRDO $MRVL $AVGO
→ Networking: $ANET $CIEN
→ Glass & fiber: $GLW
→ Manufacturing: $FN

AI demand isn't slowing. The infrastructure to support it is just getting started.

Learn the sector before it gets crowded!!!

The opportunity cost of this now is massive.

If you are relatively well off, go ahead- do your own yard work.

The entire "hard work" or "rugged" ideology was perhaps the most charming trait of the old WASP class. 

Presidents like Reagan and Bush were always being photographed cutting brush or something. 

You don't see that with Obama, Trump, etc. https://t.co/uUU3XtC0dh

text:
The Full Optical AI Industry Chain:  

1. IC Design & Manufacturing  
(Where the brains are built)

ASIC / xPU / Photonic ICs  
→ $NVDA $MRVL $AVGO $LITE $COHR $INTC $AMD $CSCO  

Wafer Foundries  
→ $TSM $TSEM $GFS $UMC  

2. Materials (The Overlooked Layer)  
(The foundation of photonics)  

Indium Phosphide  
→ $AXTI $IQE  

Gallium Arsenide  
→ $AXTI $MTSI  

This layer is underfollowed and could see strong upside as optical demand expands.

3. Optical Components  
(The data highways)  

VCSEL / EML Lasers  
→ $LITE $COHR $AVGO $AAOI  

Optical Module Assembly  
→ $FN $COHR $LITE $MRVL $CSCO  

Fiber / WDM / Optical Connectivity  
→ $CIEN $GLW $LITE  

MPO Connectors  
→ $GLW $APH  

Optical Fiber  
→ $GLW  

4. Packaging & Testing  
(The hidden backbone)  

Packaging  
→ $ASX $AMKR $FN $JBL  

Packaging Equipment  
→ $KLIC 

Testing  
→ $TER $KEYS $FORM $AEHR

As AI clusters scale into hundreds of thousands of GPUs, the challenge becomes moving data faster, farther, and with less power. Copper is hitting its limits. Optical is the next layer of scale. Why optical matters: • Higher bandwidth • Lower latency • Lower power consumption • Less heat • Better long-distance transmission That’s why hyperscalers are aggressively investing in optical-heavy infrastructure. text:
✅ قائمة المراقبة الدورية للـ AI (النسخة الكاملة)

يهذي الدورة الكبرى للذكاء الاصطناعي اللي لسى معظم الناس ما انتبهوا لها:

السيميكوندكترز (Semis): $NVDA $AMD $ARM $AVGO $MRVL $INTC $TSM $QCOM

الذاكرة (Memory): $MU $WDC $STX $SNDK

الفوتونيكس والألياف البصرية (Photonics / Optical): $LITE $COHR $AAOI $GLW $JBL $AXTI $LWLG $NOK

الشبكات (Networking): $ANET $CSCO $CIEN $JNPR

الحوسبة ومراكز البيانات (Compute / Data Centers): $IREN $CIFR $NBIS

الطاقة والشبكة الكهربائية (Power / Grid): $VRT $ETN $PWR $GEV $CEG $SMR $OKLO

المواد والمعادن (Materials / Minerals): $MP $LAC $UUUU $USAR

الفضاء والأقمار الصناعية (Space / Satellites): $ASTS $RKLB $LUNR $PL $BKSY

الدفاع والدرونز (Defense / Drones): $AVAV $KTOS $ONDS $OSS
الروبوتات والـ AI (Robotics / AI): $TSLA $PATH $SYM

هذا بالضبط مسار تدفق الفلوس ودورانها في هالسوبر سايكل.

تابعوا الدورة وما تتأخروا عن القطار 🚀

text:
$LWLG is poised for explosive growth with an unmatched competitive edge that positions the company as a clear leader in next-generation photonics for AI-driven data centers and high-speed communications. 

With over 70 issued U.S. and international patents plus dozens more pending, they enjoy complete bulletproof freedom-to-operate across their entire suite of electro-optic polymer materials and devices, eliminating any risk of IP challenges and safeguarding their path to market dominance. 

Their state-of-the-art in-house manufacturing operation right in Denver, Colorado, produces rare-earth-free, fully supply-chain-resilient polymers that are already scaled and ready for high-volume production, giving them total control over quality and eliminating the geopolitical vulnerabilities that plague competitors reliant on overseas critical materials. 

Now in 2026, the company has successfully completed its full transition from pure R&D into commercialization mode, laser-focused on delivering the exceptional reliability and thermal stability required for seamless integration into leading foundry processes like Silicon Photonics. 

At the core of this breakthrough are their proprietary Perkinamine polymers, which deliver superior electro-optic performance with blazing bandwidths beyond 200 Gbps per lane, dramatically lower power consumption, and industry-first reliability metrics that have passed rigorous Telcordia GR-468 testing. 

This powerful combination of ironclad intellectual property, domestic manufacturing independence, and polymers engineered for real-world foundry deployment creates a formidable moat that will drive significant market penetration and shareholder value as demand for ultra-efficient optical interconnects surges in the AI era.
